title: In most countries, men’s wages are higher, on average, than women’s
subtitle: A wage ratio higher than one signals higher wages for women and lower than one signals lower wages for women
source: ILO. ILOSTAT, Wages and Working Time Statistics (COND).
wage_ratio: Hourly wage ratio (Ratio of women’s to men’s hourly wages), most recent value (2015-2024)
wage_ratio_month: Monthly wage ratio (Ratio of women’s to men’s monthly wages), most recent value (2015-2024)
visdescription: This is a beeswarm where the x-axis shows World Bank regions and each dot represents the ratio of women’s average wages to men’s average wages in a country. The y-axis shows this ratio.
keyFactLabel: earned by women for every $1 earned by men in a month

scenes.text: These dynamics are reflected in women’s earnings: in the average country or economy and looking at hourly pay, they earn 91 cents for every $1 earned by men.
Of the 131 countries or economies with data, men are paid a higher hourly wage, on average, than women in 93 countries.

When measured in monthly earnings, the gap widens. As care and household responsibilities fall disproportionately on women, they are more likely to require flexible arrangements, working part-time or taking on fewer hours. So, in the average country or economy,[footnote: Using the median country as the average country] women earn just 82 cents for every dollar earned by men per month.
